claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children that I'd like to ask about - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine eligibility for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me start by asking: **How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 4 years old, Blake is 16, and Charlie is 15.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Now I need to ask: **Do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) currently live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes. Alex and Charlie live with me, but Blake doesn't live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me ask some follow-up questions about each child's situation.

Starting with Blake, who doesn't live with you: **Where is Blake currently living, and are you making any financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is living elsewhere at the moment. I do help out financially though - I contribute towards their upkeep for things like clothes and expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. To assess Blake's eligibility properly, I need to understand a bit more:

**How much do you contribute towards Blake's upkeep, and is it at least as much as the Child Benefit payment (which is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest child or £16.95 per week for additional children)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £50 a week towards Blake's upkeep, so yes, that's more than the Child Benefit payment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you, that's helpful. Now regarding Blake's situation:

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training? For example, is Blake studying at school, college, or doing an approved training course?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake recently left school and has signed up with the careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's an important detail for Blake's eligibility.

**When did Blake leave school and register with the careers service - was it within the last 20 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it was within the last 20 weeks.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Now let me ask about your other children:

**Are Alex (age 4) and Charlie (age 15) both attending school or in education, and are they both living with you full-time (not in hospital or care for extended periods)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie lives with me full-time and goes to school. Alex also lives with me and goes to nursery, but they've actually been in hospital for a couple of months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital.

**How long has Alex been in hospital - is it more or less than 12 weeks? And are you regularly spending money on Alex while they're in hospital (for example, on toys, clothes, treats, or other items)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about 12 weeks now. Yes, I am still spending money on Alex - buying toys, clothes, treats, things like that while they're in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one final question to complete my assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Good News - You Are Eligible for Child Benefit for All Three Children! 

Based on the information you've provided, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for **Alex, Blake, and Charlie**. Here's the breakdown for each child:

### **Alex (age 4)** - ✅ Eligible
Even though Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks, you continue to be entitled to Child Benefit because you are regularly spending money on Alex (toys, clothes, treats, etc.). The rules provide an exception to the 12-week hospital limit when parents continue to financially support their child.

### **Blake (age 16)** - ✅ Eligible
You can continue to claim Child Benefit for Blake for up to 20 weeks after leaving school, since Blake has registered with a careers service. Additionally, because you're contributing £50 per week towards Blake's upkeep (which is more than the Child Benefit amount), you are considered responsible for Blake even though they don't live with you.

### **Charlie (age 15)** - ✅ Eligible
Charlie lives with you, attends school, and is under 16, so you clearly qualify for Child Benefit.

---

**Important note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge. You can use the Child Benefit tax calculator on GOV.UK to check if this applies to you.

If you haven't already claimed Child Benefit, you should make a claim. If you have any other questions, please feel free to ask!

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old and lives with you in the UK. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, you are regularly spending money on Alex (buying toys, clothes, treats, etc.). Under the rules, there is an exception to the 12-week hospital limit for children in UK hospitals when the parent regularly spends money on them. Therefore, you remain entitled to Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old and recently left school and registered with a government-sponsored careers service within the last 20 weeks. Under the rules, you can claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks for a 16 or 17 year old who leaves education and registers with a careers service. Although Blake does not live with you, you are contributing £50 per week towards Blake's upkeep, which is more than the Child Benefit payment amount. This means you are still responsible for Blake under the rules, as you are paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 15 years old, lives with you full-time in the UK, and attends school. You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you, and they are under 16. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has three children: Alex (4), Blake (16), and Charlie (15). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them. Charlie clearly qualifies as they live with the claimant and attend school. Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks, but the claimant regularly spends money on them, which is an exception to the 12-week hospital rule. Blake doesn't live with the claimant but the claimant contributes £50/week towards their upkeep (more than the Child Benefit amount), and Blake left school and registered with the careers service within the last 20 weeks, making them eligible under the 20-week extension rule. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children.

Charlie clearly qualifies as they live with the claimant and attend school.
